pages.text:
335
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permission
to photocopy this handout is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details).
Distress Tolerance Handout 8a (Distress Tolerance Worksheet 6c)
Body Scan Meditation Step by Step
Sit on a chair, or lie on your back on the floor with legs uncrossed. Put your arms in a comfortable
position by your side, on your abdomen, or (if sitting) put them on your thighs palms up. Open your
eyes partially to let light in. If you are lying on the floor, put a cushion under your knees if need
be. Imagine your breath flowing to each part of your body as your attention gently moves up your
body. Adopt a mind of curiosity and interest as you focus on each part of your body.
Focus on your breathing. Notice how the air moves in and out of your body.
•• Take several deep breaths until you begin to feel comfortable and relaxed.
•• Direct your attention to the toes of your left foot.
•• Notice the sensations in that part of your body while remaining aware of your breathing.
•• Imagine each breath flowing to your toes.
•• Looking with curiosity, ask, “What am I feeling in this part of my body?”
•• Focus on your left toes for several minutes.
•• Then move your focus to the arch and heel of your left foot, and hold it there for a minute or
two while continuing to pay attention to your breathing.
•• Notice the sensations on your skin of warmth or coldness; notice the weight of your foot on
the floor.
•• Imagine your breath flowing to the arch and heel of your left foot.
•• Ask, “What are the feelings in the arch and heel of my left foot?”
•• Follow the same procedure as you move to your left ankle, calf, knee, upper legs, and thigh.
•• Repeat with the right leg, starting with your toes.
•• Then move through your pelvis, and lower back, and around to your stomach.
•• Focus on the rising and falling of your belly as your breath goes in and out.
•• Then go on to your chest; left hand, arm, and shoulder; right hand, arm, and shoulder; neck,
chin, tongue, mouth, lips, and lower face; and nose.
•• Notice your breath as it comes in and out of your nostrils.
•• Then focus on your upper cheeks, eyes, forehead, and scalp.
•• Finally, focus on the very top of your hair.
•• Then let go of your body altogether.
Don’t worry if you notice that thoughts, sounds, or other sensations come into your awareness.
Just notice them and then gently refocus your mind. Don’t worry if your mind has been drawn away
from the object of your attention and you find yourself thinking about something else (it nearly
always happens). Just calmly, gently, but with resolution, turn your mind back to the part of the body
you’ve reached. You may need to bring your attention back over and over. You are not alone in this.
It is this bringing of your attention back over and over and over, without judgment or harshness, that
is the essential element of the meditation.

Distress Tolerance Handout 8a (Distress Tolerance Worksheet 6c)
Body Scan Meditation Step by Step
Sit on a chair, or lie on your back on the floor with legs uncrossed. Put your arms in a comfortable
position by your side, on your abdomen, or (if sitting) put them on your thighs palms up. Open your
eyes partially to let light in. If you are lying on the floor, put a cushion under your knees if need
be. Imagine your breath flowing to each part of your body as your attention gently moves up your
body. Adopt a mind of curiosity and interest as you focus on each part of your body.
Focus on your breathing. Notice how the air moves in and out of your body.
•• Take several deep breaths until you begin to feel comfortable and relaxed.
•• Direct your attention to the toes of your left foot.
•• Notice the sensations in that part of your body while remaining aware of your breathing.
•• Imagine each breath flowing to your toes.
•• Looking with curiosity, ask, “What am I feeling in this part of my body?”
•• Focus on your left toes for several minutes.
•• Then move your focus to the arch and heel of your left foot, and hold it there for a minute or
two while continuing to pay attention to your breathing.
•• Notice the sensations on your skin of warmth or coldness; notice the weight of your foot on
the floor.
•• Imagine your breath flowing to the arch and heel of your left foot.
•• Ask, “What are the feelings in the arch and heel of my left foot?”
•• Follow the same procedure as you move to your left ankle, calf, knee, upper legs, and thigh.
•• Repeat with the right leg, starting with your toes.
•• Then move through your pelvis, and lower back, and around to your stomach.
•• Focus on the rising and falling of your belly as your breath goes in and out.
•• Then go on to your chest; left hand, arm, and shoulder; right hand, arm, and shoulder; neck,
chin, tongue, mouth, lips, and lower face; and nose.
•• Notice your breath as it comes in and out of your nostrils.
•• Then focus on your upper cheeks, eyes, forehead, and scalp.
•• Finally, focus on the very top of your hair.
•• Then let go of your body altogether.

It is this bringing of your attention back over and over and over, without judgment or harshness, that
is the essential element of the meditation.

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Body Scan Meditation Step by Step Sit on a chair, or lie on your back on the floor with legs uncrossed. Put your arms in a comfortable position by your side, on your abdomen, or (if sitting) put them on your thighs palms up. Open your eyes partially to let light in. If you are lying on the floor, put a cushion under your knees if need

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Choose one sentence or tool that fits today.
Use the low-spoon version; no full worksheet is required.

routine_integration.routine_role: monthly review or larger pattern tool
routine_integration.start_the_day_use: not auto-loaded on survival days; use as a scheduled monthly review item
routine_integration.recommended_block: monthly support audit or household reset
